era_context: The Ming Dynasty is in the midst of the Jingnan Rebellion (1399–1402), with the Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di) challenging his nephew, the Jianwen Emperor. The Ottoman Empire is at the height of its power under Bayezid I. In Europe, the Hundred Years' War continues; the Western Schism in the Catholic Church persists. The Italian Renaissance is beginning to flourish, with humanist scholars and artists reshaping European culture. This year marks the beginning of the 15th century.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (limited written records)

events.title: Owain Glyndŵr Declares Himself Prince of Wales; Rebellion Begins
events.description: Owain Glyndŵr (c. 1359–c. 1415), a Welsh nobleman, declares himself Prince of Wales and launches a rebellion against English rule. The rebellion is triggered by a personal dispute with an English neighbor, but it quickly becomes a nationalist uprising against English authority. Glyndŵr's rebellion will last until 1415 and will be one of the last major Welsh uprisings against English rule. The rebellion attracts support from various Welsh lords and common people, and at its height, Glyndŵr controls much of Wales. The rebellion poses a significant challenge to Henry IV's authority and diverts English military resources.
events.certainty_note: Glyndŵr's rebellion is attested in contemporary English chronicles and later Welsh sources. The date of 1400 is generally accepted for the beginning of the rebellion.

events.title: Jingnan Rebellion Begins; Yongle Emperor Challenges Jianwen Emperor
events.description: The Jingnan Rebellion (1399–1402) begins, with the Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di), Prince of Yan, challenging his nephew, the Jianwen Emperor, for control of the Ming Dynasty. The rebellion is triggered by the Jianwen Emperor's attempts to reduce the power of the regional princes, which threatens the Yongle Emperor's position. The Yongle Emperor raises an army and marches on Nanjing, the capital. The rebellion will last three years and will result in the Yongle Emperor's victory and consolidation of power. This rebellion is one of the most significant civil conflicts in Ming history and has profound implications for the dynasty's future.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Jingnan Rebellion is well-documented in Ming administrative records and later historical compilations. The date of 1399 is sometimes given for the start of the rebellion, but 1400 is also used.

events.title: Henry IV of England Consolidates Power; Early Reign Challenges
events.description: Henry IV of England (r. 1399–1413) is in his first year of rule after deposing Richard II in 1399. By 1400, Henry is working to consolidate his power and establish his legitimacy. He faces multiple challenges: the Glyndŵr rebellion in Wales, baronial opposition, religious dissent (Lollards), and questions about the legitimacy of his claim to the throne. Despite these challenges, Henry is gradually establishing his authority. The year 1400 marks the beginning of a reign characterized by constant vigilance against potential rivals and rebels.
events.certainty_note: Henry IV's consolidation of power is well-documented in contemporary English sources.

events.title: Ottoman Empire at Peak Power; Bayezid I Rules
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Sultan Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402) is at the height of its power and territorial expansion. By 1400, the Ottomans control much of Anatolia and the Balkans, and they are threatening Constantinople, the last major Byzantine stronghold. Bayezid I is known as 'the Thunderbolt' (Yildirim) for his military prowess and rapid campaigns. However, by 1400, Timur's invasion is imminent, and within two years, Bayezid will be defeated at the Battle of Ankara and captured. The Ottoman Empire will be temporarily fragmented, but it will eventually recover and become the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Bayezid I's rule and the Ottoman Empire's power are well-documented in Ottoman and Byzantine sources.

events.title: Venice Maintains Maritime Dominance; Commercial Networks Expand
events.description: Venice, the dominant maritime power in the Mediterranean, is maintaining and expanding its commercial networks. The Republic is at the height of its power and influence, with a sophisticated commercial and financial system. Venice's wealth is based on its control of Mediterranean trade, particularly the spice trade from the East. By 1400, Venice is one of the wealthiest and most powerful city-states in Europe. The Republic is engaged in diplomatic negotiations with various powers and is securing trade routes and establishing trading posts throughout the Mediterranean and beyond.

sources.name: Venetian state archives (Archivio di Stato di Venezia)

events.certainty_note: Venetian commercial and political dominance is well-documented in state archives and contemporary sources.

events.title: Italian Renaissance Flourishes; Humanist Scholarship Advances
events.description: The Italian Renaissance is flourishing in 1400, with humanist scholars and artists reshaping European culture. Florence is the center of this cultural movement, with the Medici family beginning to emerge as patrons of the arts and learning. Humanist scholars are recovering and studying classical texts, and artists are developing new techniques in painting, sculpture, and architecture. The Renaissance represents a revival of interest in classical learning and a shift away from medieval scholasticism. By 1400, the movement is well-established in Italy and is beginning to spread to other parts of Europe.

sources.name: Giorgio Vasari's Lives of the Artists

events.certainty_note: The Italian Renaissance is well-documented in contemporary sources and later historical accounts.

events.title: Portuguese Exploration Begins; Prince Henry the Navigator's Early Patronage
events.description: Portuguese exploration of the Atlantic Ocean is beginning under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator (1394–1460). By 1400, Portuguese expeditions have reached the Canary Islands and are beginning to explore the West African coast. These voyages are motivated by commercial interests (gold, spices, slaves) and religious zeal (conversion of non-Christians). The Portuguese are developing new maritime technologies and navigation techniques that will eventually enable the Age of Exploration. Prince Henry's patronage of exploration will continue for decades and will establish Portugal as a leading maritime power.

sources.name: Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea by Gomes Eanes de Zurara

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration is attested in later chronicles; specific dates for individual voyages in 1400 are uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of the beginning of the Jingnan Rebellion is sometimes given as 1399 rather than 1400; modern scholarship generally accepts 1399 as the start date, with 1400 being the year of major military campaigns. Owain Glyndŵr's rebellion is sometimes portrayed as a purely nationalist uprising, but it was also driven by personal grievances and feudal disputes. The significance of the rebellion is sometimes overstated; while it was a major uprising, it did not fundamentally alter the balance of power between Wales and England. The Italian Renaissance is sometimes portrayed as a sudden break from the medieval period, but it was actually a gradual process that had been developing for decades. Prince Henry the Navigator's role in Portuguese exploration is sometimes romanticized; he was one patron among many, and Portuguese exploration was driven by multiple economic and political factors.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1400 is moderate for Europe and China, with administrative records and chronicles available. Ottoman sources are less abundant but still available. Italian sources are detailed regarding Florence and Venice. African, American, and Oceanic history remains almost entirely absent from surviving written records. The sources that do exist are often produced by and for the ruling classes, potentially obscuring the experiences of common people, women, and non-literate societies. Chinese sources may be biased toward the Yongle Emperor's legitimacy, while English sources may downplay the significance of Glyndŵr's rebellion. The Italian Renaissance is often portrayed through the lens of elite patrons and artists, potentially obscuring the experiences of common people and women.

graph_edges.note: Glyndŵr's rebellion begins in 1400 and continues until 1415
Jingnan Rebellion begins in 1400 and leads to Yongle Emperor's consolidation in 1402
Ottoman Empire's peak power in 1400 is followed by defeat at Ankara in 1402
Italian Renaissance flourishes in 1400 and continues to develop throughout the 15th century
